summary: English Renaissance composer (c. 1543–1623), student of Thomas Tallis at the Chapel Royal. Catholic composer who prospered under the Protestant Elizabethan regime. With Tallis, held a 21-year royal monopoly on music printing. Prolific composer of keyboard music (especially for the virginal), motets, masses, and consort songs. Known as 'Britanniae Musicae Parens' (Father of British Music).

works.name: My Ladye Nevells Booke
works.description: A collection of 42 keyboard works dedicated to "My Ladye Nevell," including ten paired pavanes and galliards. One of the most important collections of keyboard music from the English Renaissance, alongside the Fitzwilliam Virginal Book. Contains works such as "The Battle" (a nine-movement battaglia) and "The Barley Break." Wikipedia

works.name: Cantiones Sacrae
works.description: A collection of Latin motets co-published with Thomas Tallis in 1575, dedicated to Queen Elizabeth I, who granted the two composers their 21-year monopoly on music printing. Byrd also published his own later sets of Cantiones Sacrae (1589, 1591). Wikipedia

clues.clue: The Cantiones Sacrae monopoly was the first-ever letters patent on music printing
examples: The joint work Cantiones Sacrae resulted from the first-ever letters patent on printing music.

works.name: Thomas Tallis (teacher and collaborator)
works.description: Thomas Tallis (c. 1505–1585) was Byrd's teacher at the Chapel Royal. The two shared a royal monopoly on music printing from Queen Elizabeth I. Tallis composed the famous 40-part motet Spem in Alium. Byrd wrote the elegy Ye Sacred Muses on Tallis's death. Tallis's hymn melody later formed the basis of Vaughan Williams's Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis. Wikipedia

works.name: Ave Verum Corpus
works.description: A motet in G Aeolian mode (G minor). Notable for stressing 'verum' rather than 'corpus,' and for using a 'false relation' between an F-sharp in the Superius and an F in the Bassus. Written in 4/2 time. Wikipedia

works.name: Gradualia
works.description: A 109-piece collection providing complete Mass Propers for the major feasts of the Roman liturgy, published in two volumes (1605, 1607). Remarkable given Byrd's precarious position as a Catholic under the Protestant Elizabethan regime. Wikipedia

works.name: Mass Settings (Three Masses)
works.description: Byrd composed three complete Mass settings, for three, four, and five voices respectively. His Mass for Four Voices is the most widely known. These were published without title pages, likely because Catholic liturgical music was sensitive under Protestant rule. Wikipedia

works.name: Earl of Salisbury Pavans / Keyboard Works
works.description: A pair of dances (pavan and galliard) composed for Robert Cecil, 1st Earl of Salisbury, inspired by his death. Byrd also wrote the earliest known hexachord fantasia, and composed variations on popular tunes. The Fitzwilliam Virginal Book contains further keyboard works. Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: William Byrd (c. 1543–1623) was an English Renaissance composer, organist at the Chapel Royal, and devout Catholic who nonetheless prospered under Protestant Queen Elizabeth I. He was a student of Thomas Tallis, and the most notable composer of consort songs. Known as 'Britanniae Musicae Parens.' Byrd's Catholicism is reflected in his liturgical works despite his precarious position.

comprehensive_summary: William Byrd (c. 1543–1623) was the foremost English composer of the Renaissance, known by the honorific 'Britanniae Musicae Parens' (Father of British Music). He studied under Thomas Tallis at the Chapel Royal in London, and together the two were granted a 21-year royal monopoly on music printing by Queen Elizabeth I — the first-ever letters patent on music printing — leading to their jointly published Cantiones Sacrae (1575), a collection of Latin motets dedicated to the queen. Despite being a devout Catholic in Protestant England, Byrd managed to prosper under Elizabeth I's patronage. His keyboard works are central to his legacy: My Ladye Nevells Booke (1591) is a collection of 42 keyboard pieces dedicated to Lady Nevell, containing ten paired pavanes and galliards, variations on popular tunes like 'Carman's Whistle' and 'Sellinger's Round,' the song 'Will You Walk the Woods so Wild,' a hexachord fantasia, and the nine-movement battaglia 'The Battle' (whose fourth section includes 'the marche of horsemen' and 'the flute & the drome'). Alongside the Fitzwilliam Virginal Book (in which Byrd appears with John Bull, Peter Philips, and Giles Farnaby), My Ladye Nevells Booke is considered the most important collection of English Renaissance keyboard music. Byrd also contributed two pavanes and four galliards to Parthenia, the first printed collection of keyboard music in England. He composed three complete Mass settings (for three, four, and five voices), which circulated without title pages under the dangerous Catholic climate. His Gradualia (two volumes, 1605–07) provided 109 pieces of Mass Propers for the major feasts of the Roman liturgy. His motet Ave Verum Corpus is written in G Aeolian (G minor) and stresses 'verum' rather than 'corpus.' Its opening moves through G minor, D major, and then the unexpected F major, creating an F-sharp/F-natural cross-relation between the superius and bass; a similar false relation appears at the close on the repeated words 'Miserere mei.' The motet sets a 14th-century Latin Eucharistic text whose opening words translate as 'Hail, true body.' On Tallis's death, Byrd composed the elegy Ye Sacred Muses, concluding with 'Tallis is dead, and Music dies.' He preceded John Bull and Jan Sweelinck in composing the earliest known hexachord fantasia, he wrote a lullaby (berceuse), and he taught Thomas Morley, author of the Plain and Easy Introduction to Practical Music. A pair of dances was inspired by the death of Robert Cecil, Earl of Salisbury. Gordon Jacob later composed a suite 'freely transcribing' six of Byrd's pieces, more famous in its version for symphonic wind band.

Fitzwilliam Virginal Book — major keyboard anthology containing Byrd's works alongside John Bull, Peter Philips, and Giles Farnaby
Ralph Vaughan Williams — composed Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis, frequently mentioned as a downstream consequence of the Tallis–Byrd connection
